publications.editedComment: Program activities are measurable. The canvas seeds ordinary pellets, spawns bugs under a five-bug cap, and runs one Pac-Bot with position, velocity, score, mouth cycle, trail, and timed power state. Pointer movement drops short-lived cursor pellets after enough travel. Clicking funds one power pellet and sparkles; double-clicking schedules a twenty-crumb burst. Distance checks remove collected pellets and bugs, update score, and create feedback text. The lifecycle budget includes resize, pointer, click, double-click, and animation cleanup. A grant reviewer would flag the uncancelled crumb-storm timeouts as residual obligations, but the core outcomes—inputs, caps, scoring, and visual feedback—are plainly documented in code.

This plasma is deliberately coarse metal. The backing canvas renders at one-sixth the viewport width and height, then CSS scales it up with pixelated smoothing disabled. Every frame visits each reduced-resolution pixel, combines four sine fields across x, y, diagonal distance, and radial distance, converts the resulting animated hue from HSL to RGB, and writes it into one reused ImageData buffer at alpha 80. The buffer is pushed directly to the canvas and the loop repeats. Resize updates both the low-resolution drawing surface and full-size CSS dimensions. The frame and resize listener are cleaned up. Nothing reacts to input; performance comes from fewer pixels, not hidden acceleration.

PIXELS WAVE SMILE! The Component divides viewport dimensions by six, creates one ImageData buffer for that reduced canvas, and reuses it every frame. Four sine terms produce a changing value for every pixel; that value becomes an animated hue, `hslToRgb` supplies the channels, and alpha stays fixed at 80. CSS stretches the result to the viewport with pixelated rendering, trading detail for a much smaller per-frame loop. Resize rebuilds the drawing dimensions, and unmount cancels the frame and listener. No pointer, store, or random state changes the pattern. It is transparent about the trick: low resolution, layered waves, bright motion. :)

Tea's warm; the water keeps its records. Cursor travel accumulates until sixty pixels have passed, then creates a cursor ripple. Mouse-down creates a larger splash. Ambient droplets fall from above and become smaller drip ripples near ninety-two percent of the screen height. Each ripple type has separate radius, speed, decay, hue, and concentric-ring ranges. The frame paints a dark water fade, expands and removes rings, adds inner shimmer, and marks near-intersections between overlapping circles. Resize, mousemove, and mousedown listeners are all removed with the frame on unmount. Nothing is stored after the Component leaves. A well-tended pond should know exactly when to clear its cups.

The interaction record distinguishes three ripple classes. Cursor ripples appear after accumulated pointer travel, splash ripples on mouse-down, and drip ripples when scheduled droplets reach the lower waterline. Each class carries historically fixed ranges for maximum radius, decay, expansion speed, hue, and ring count. Frames retain a dark tint, render concentric rings and highlights, remove expired entries, and calculate a small shimmer where expanding circles approach an intersection. The implementation also documents its lifecycle correctly: resize and mouse handlers are attached to window, then removed, and requestAnimationFrame is cancelled. No persistent timeline remains; the history is visible only in the fading rings.

This canvas arrives already frosted with forty large color blobs, eighty directional strokes, a hundred twenty bright flecks, and a dark vignette. Dragging normally samples pixels from behind the brush and redraws them through a circular clipping area, producing the smudge. Holding Shift changes the drag into a hue-cycling radial paint dab. The mouse wheel sizes the brush from 8 to 120 pixels, touch supports smudging, and the custom cursor shows radius and paint state. Resize preserves the previous canvas before stretching it into the new dimensions. The hint disappears after four seconds. Every listener and that hint timer are cleaned up. Sticky, colorful, and honestly labeled candy-work.

This one captures pointer events on purpose, friend. The starting field is generated from layered gradients, brush strokes, flecks, and a vignette. Mouse drag smudges by reading a bounded pixel sample into a reusable temporary canvas and drawing it forward under a soft circular clip. Shift-drag paints a changing radial hue instead. Wheel adjusts radius, touch gestures smudge, and the visible brush ring tracks position and mode. Resize copies the old canvas before resizing so the work survives. A four-second help banner explains the controls. Canvas, window, touch, keyboard, wheel, resize, and timer cleanup are all explicit. Foggy colors, unusually clear lifecycle.

Two hundred twenty stars begin in three-dimensional coordinates around the viewport center, each holding current and previous depth. Every frame moves depth five units toward the viewer. A star crossing the near plane is reassigned a random x and y and placed at the far width. Perspective projection converts both depth positions into screen coordinates, so the canvas draws a line from the prior point to the current point. Nearer stars become brighter and thicker. A translucent navy repaint leaves the sensation of speed without a separate trail collection. Resize updates the canvas, and unmount cancels the frame and listener. No wire, no input, only repeated projection keeping the fall controlled.

Why did the star cross the screen? Because its z value hit one and the code sent it back to the far side. There are 220 of these commuters. Each frame subtracts five from depth, projects the current and previous positions around the canvas center, and draws the gap as a streak. Distance controls brightness and line width, while a translucent dark repaint preserves a short trail. Recycled stars get fresh x and y coordinates but no personality questionnaire. Resize changes the canvas dimensions; unmount removes the listener and cancels the frame. It is a classic warp field with excellent turnover and absolutely no baggage fees.
